content.text: When you call Claude through the Anthropic API or a cloud provider's managed service, the request is processed in a specific data center region. Understanding where computation happens matters for latency, data residency compliance (GDPR, HIPAA, data sovereignty laws), and high-availability architecture.
There are three deployment paths: the
(AWS-hosted, multiple regions with cross-region inference), and
(GCP-hosted, multiple regions). Microsoft Azure is also supported in some tiers.

Choosing where to call Claude is also choosing where your data is processed and which compliance frameworks govern it. The Direct API and cloud provider deployments have meaningfully different data handling guarantees.

attrs.alt: Claude API regional infrastructure showing AWS Bedrock, GCP Vertex AI, and Azure regions across North America, Europe, and Asia Pacific

content.text: ) is hosted on Anthropic's infrastructure. All requests are processed in Anthropic-controlled facilities — there is currently
no multi-region failover or customer-selectable region
. Anthropic handles availability and redundancy internally.
No published uptime SLA on free/paid tiers
Prototyping, tools without strict residency needs
If you need a contractual data residency commitment, the cloud provider integrations (Bedrock, Vertex AI) are the right path because they give you the provider's DPA and processing agreements.

Amazon Bedrock serves Claude in multiple AWS regions. You choose the region by calling the regional endpoint rather than a global one. This is the most feature-complete managed option with the widest regional footprint.
To use a Bedrock region, set your

import boto3
import json

# Specify the region explicitly
bedrock = boto3.client(
    service_name="bedrock-runtime",
    region_name="eu-west-1",     # data stays in Ireland
)

body = json.dumps({
    "anthropic_version": "bedrock-2023-05-31",
    "max_tokens": 1024,
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hello from the EU region"}],
})

response = bedrock.invoke_model(
    modelId="anthropic.claude-opus-4-8",
    body=body,
)
result = json.loads(response["body"].read())
print(result["content"][0]["text"])
— logical endpoints that automatically route requests within a geography (US, EU, APAC) to whichever region has capacity. This raises effective throughput limits without you managing failover logic.

# Use a cross-region inference profile ID instead of a model ID
response = bedrock.invoke_model(
    modelId="us.anthropic.claude-opus-4-8-20251101-v1:0",   # US cross-region
    body=body,
)
Cross-region profiles are prefixed with the geo:
. Data stays within the named geography even when routed to a different AWS region in that geo.
Google Cloud Vertex AI also hosts Claude in multiple GCP regions. Access uses the Vertex AI SDK or the Anthropic Python SDK with the

import anthropic

# Install: pip install anthropic[vertex]
client = anthropic.AnthropicVertex(
    region="europe-west4",            # GDPR-compliant EU region
    project_id="my-gcp-project",
)

message = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-8@20251101",  # Vertex model IDs use @ version suffix
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello from Vertex AI Europe"}],
)
print(message.content[0].text)
suffix used by Bedrock and the Direct API.
Data residency is the primary driver of region choice in regulated industries. The matrix below maps common compliance frameworks to the deployment choice that satisfies them:
Data stays in EU; provider DPA available
AWS HIPAA-eligible services; BAA covers Bedrock
Direct API or any provider region

Availability of specific models varies by region and changes over time. Always consult the provider console (AWS Bedrock model catalog, Vertex AI model garden) before committing to a region in production.
Network round-trip time adds to every Claude call. Choose the region geographically closest to your servers to minimize this:
adds a routing hop but the throughput gain usually outweighs the small latency penalty
Time-to-first-token latency is more region-sensitive than total response time because the network RTT is paid before any tokens are streamed. Benchmark your target region early in development.
Not every Claude model is available in every region at launch. Newer models often debut in high-capacity US regions (
) and roll out to other regions over weeks or months.

import boto3

# List Claude models available in a given Bedrock region
bedrock = boto3.client("bedrock", region_name="eu-west-1")
response = bedrock.list_foundation_models(byProvider="Anthropic")

for model in response["modelSummaries"]:
    print(model["modelId"], "—", model["modelName"])
API or the model garden UI shows which Claude model versions are available in each region. Always pin a specific version (e.g.
) rather than a floating alias in production.

The Direct API provides no multi-region failover at the caller level. For HA on the Direct API, implement your own retry logic with exponential backoff and accept that a regional outage will surface as temporary errors.

Bedrock's cross-region inference profiles provide the simplest HA story for cloud-hosted deployments: Bedrock handles routing within the geo automatically. If you need geo-level HA (US + EU), you must deploy two stacks and route at the application layer.

Anthropic Claude is available on Microsoft Azure AI Services (formerly Azure OpenAI). Azure availability is currently more limited than Bedrock or Vertex AI, but is expanding. The integration is useful for organizations already deeply invested in the Azure ecosystem and who use Azure for their compliance boundaries.

# Using the Azure AI Inference SDK
from azure.ai.inference import ChatCompletionsClient
from azure.core.credentials import AzureKeyCredential

client = ChatCompletionsClient(
    endpoint="https://<your-endpoint>.openai.azure.com",
    credential=AzureKeyCredential("<your-api-key>"),
)

response = client.complete(
    model="claude-opus-4-8",   # deployment name in Azure
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello from Azure East US"}],
    max_tokens=1024,
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)
Alternatively, the Anthropic Python SDK supports Azure via the

pattern — check the SDK docs for the current Azure-specific client. Azure deployments use your Azure subscription billing and comply with Microsoft's DPA, making them suitable for organizations with an existing enterprise agreement with Microsoft.

Prompt Caching (available on Claude 3+ via the Direct API and Bedrock) stores large, repeated context blocks server-side to reduce input tokens and cost on subsequent requests. Cache behavior is region-scoped: a cache written in
. If you serve multiple regions, each region maintains its own prompt cache.
Prompt Cache TTL is 5 minutes by default (extended to 1 hour on Bedrock with extended cache). In a multi-region setup,
during startup to avoid cache-miss latency spikes at request time.
Data Residency: What Actually Leaves the Region
"Data stays in the region" is nuanced. Here is what actually happens in each deployment path:

Model weights are replicated to each region by the cloud provider — you do not send weights, they do. Anthropic trains the model; the provider receives a licensed copy and stores it within the region. Training data and fine-tuning (if used) have separate residency considerations governed by the provider's service terms.

The cloud provider acts as a Data Processor for the inference request/response. Your organization is the Data Controller. Ensure you have a signed DPA with the provider before processing personal data.
Production deployments need per-region metrics to detect latency degradation, elevated error rates, or quota exhaustion. The right approach differs by deployment path:

import anthropic, time, statistics

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def timed_call(messages):
    start = time.perf_counter()
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=messages,
    )
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - start
    return response, elapsed

# Collect latency samples
latencies = []
for _ in range(10):
    _, t = timed_call([{"role": "user", "content": "ping"}])
    latencies.append(t)

print(f"p50: {statistics.median(latencies):.2f}s")
print(f"p95: {sorted(latencies)[int(len(latencies)*0.95)]:.2f}s")
Bedrock automatically emits CloudWatch metrics per region including
with a threshold appropriate for your SLA. Route to SNS or PagerDuty for on-call alerting.

import boto3

cw = boto3.client("cloudwatch", region_name="eu-west-1")

# Create alarm for error rate
cw.put_metric_alarm(
    AlarmName="claude-bedrock-errors-eu",
    MetricName="InvocationErrors",
    Namespace="AWS/Bedrock",
    Statistic="Sum",
    Period=60,
    EvaluationPeriods=3,
    Threshold=5,
    ComparisonOperator="GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold",
    Dimensions=[
        {"Name": "ModelId", "Value": "anthropic.claude-opus-4-8"},
        {"Name": "Region", "Value": "eu-west-1"},
    ],
    AlarmActions=["arn:aws:sns:eu-west-1:123456789:claude-alerts"],
)

Each region has its own quota bucket: tokens-per-minute (TPM), requests-per-minute (RPM), and sometimes concurrent-request limits. Bedrock quotas are configured per region in the AWS Service Quotas console. Quota exhaustion in one region does not affect another.
: run all traffic in your primary region, but configure a secondary region at lower cost. If the primary returns 429 (rate limited) or 529 (overloaded), redirect the call to the secondary:
Cross-region inference profiles automate this pattern within a geography. The manual approach above is needed if you want cross-geography failover (e.g. US → EU as last resort).

on the Direct API — you pay the same per-token rate regardless of where Anthropic routes your request. On Bedrock, pricing is typically identical across regions for the same model, but always verify in the Bedrock pricing page as new regions or promotional pricing may differ.
vary by region is data transfer cost. On AWS,
data transfer into Bedrock is free

; response data transferred out of the AWS region to the internet incurs standard AWS outbound data transfer fees (~$0.09/GB for the first 10 TB). Place your application in the same AWS region as Bedrock to eliminate inter-region data transfer costs.
: Inbound free; outbound at AWS internet egress rates
: Cache writes cost 25% of base input price; cache reads cost 10% — identical across regions

Claude is available through three deployment paths: the Direct API (Anthropic-hosted, simple, no region choice), Amazon Bedrock (multi-region, cross-region inference, HIPAA/GDPR-eligible), and Google Cloud Vertex AI (multi-region, GCP DPA). Choose your region based on latency, data residency requirements, and the compliance framework your application must satisfy. Model availability varies by region and changes over time, so always verify in the provider console before committing to a region. For high availability, Bedrock's cross-region inference profiles are the lowest-effort option.
